title: Possession, čí, and demonstratives
summary: Say who owns something and distinguish identifying to from gendered demonstratives.
objectives: Choose singular possessive pronouns by noun gender
Ask and answer ownership questions with čí
Use ten, ta, to and tenhle, tahle, tohle
Distinguish unchanging To je from demonstratives before nouns

sections.heading: Possessive pronouns
sections.body: Můj, tvůj, náš, and váš agree with the gender of the possessed noun. Jeho, její, and jejich normally do not change.
examples: můj kamarád / moje kamarádka / moje auto
náš profesor / naše profesorka / naše město
jeho kniha / její auto / jejich pes

sections.heading: Asking with čí
sections.body: Use the memorized frame Čí je to ___? Czech uses the same possessive form for English my and mine, your and yours, and so on.
examples: Čí je to pes? — Je můj.
Čí je to kniha? — Je moje.
Čí je to auto? — Je jeho.

sections.heading: Demonstratives before nouns
sections.body: Ten, ta, to agree with a following masculine, feminine, or neuter noun. Add -hle for a specifically nearby this.
examples: ten stůl / ta profesorka / to pivo
tenhle klíč / tahle židle / tohle auto

sections.heading: Unchanging To je
sections.body: When identifying something with This is or That is, use To je regardless of the noun's gender. Use ten, ta, to only when the demonstrative directly modifies a noun.

resources.title: Possessive pronouns
resources.note: Singular possessive forms and gender agreement.

resources.title: Demonstrative pronouns
resources.note: Ten, ta, to and nearby -hle forms.

exercises.explanation: Kamarádka is feminine, so use moje.

exercises.prompt: Type the Czech question for: Whose book is this?
exercises.explanation: Use the fixed frame Čí je to plus the noun.
Pivo is neuter, so the demonstrative before it is to.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence means This is a new computer?
